title: Simple sign epistasis and evolutionary detours in fitness landscapes
authors: Paolo Ribeca, Alejandro Castro, Alejandro Lage-Castellanos, Alisa Sergeeva, Sebastian Matuszewski, Ruben Gustavo Paccosi, Vitaly Belik, Mahan Ghafari, Joachim Krug, Guillaume Achaz, Luca Ferretti
abstract: In epistatic fitness landscapes, the fitness effect of a mutation depends on the genetic background and may even switch between deleterious and beneficial depending on the presence of another mutation. Epistatic interactions may cause both mutations to change the sign of each other's fitness effects (reciprocal sign epistasis) or only one mutation to do so (simple sign epistasis). Both these forms of epistasis influence evolutionary trajectories. While reciprocal sign epistasis has been associated with multi-peaked landscapes and their ruggedness, the role and relative frequency of simple sign epistasis in fitness landscapes have not been systematically investigated. Here, we prove that the presence of simple sign epistasis is associated with evolutionary detours, i.e., indirect, longer fitness-increasing paths to fitness peaks that include back-mutations. We also show that in experimentally resolved, weakly epistatic landscapes, simple sign epistasis occurs much more frequently than reciprocal sign epistasis. This result is consistent with the theoretical predictions we derive for most landscape models, with the exception of the block model and of landscapes dominated by pairwise allelic incompatibilities, such as RNA stability landscapes. Our results suggest that detours represent a general feature of evolutionary trajectories in weakly epistatic landscapes.
summary: This paper explores how different types of genetic interactions, known as epistasis, shape the paths evolution takes through genotype space. It identifies that 'simple sign epistasis' is a key driver of evolutionary detours, where organisms must temporarily move through less-fit states to reach higher peaks. While most landscapes are dominated by this type of interaction, the authors also identify specific structured models where reciprocal interactions can dominate. The findings bridge theoretical landscape models with real-world biological observations.
main_problem: The paper investigates the relative frequency and evolutionary impact of simple sign epistasis (SSE) versus reciprocal sign epistasis (RSE) in fitness landscapes.
main_result: The authors prove that SSE is the necessary and sufficient condition for evolutionary detours (paths involving back-mutations) and show that SSE is much more frequent than RSE in weakly epistatic, experimentally resolved landscapes.
method: The study combines analytical derivations using Gaussian integration and mean-field approximations with large-scale simulations and statistical analysis of empirical landscapes (e.g., MAGELLAN, ViennaRNA).
model_or_system: The paper analyzes various fitness landscape models, including House of Cards (HoC), Rough Mount Fuji (RMF), NK models, and spin-glass-like pairwise allelic incompatibility models.
key_observables: Fractions of epistatic motifs (phi_m, phi_ss, phi_rs), correlation of fitness effects (gamma), and accessibility measures (direct vs. indirect paths).
important_parameters: Interaction regimes (dense vs. sparse), scaled slope (s/sigma), and interaction degree (I).
main_assumptions: Many analytical derivations assume Gaussian-distributed fitness effects and permutation invariance (unstructured landscapes).
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes ME, SSE, and RSE motifs and their fitness graphs; Figure 2 compares theoretical predictions to simulations; Figure 4 compares empirical RSE/SSE fractions; Figure 7 analyzes the RNA secondary structure landscape.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the types of epistasis, provides mathematical proofs for the link between SSE and detours, derives analytical predictions for various landscape models, compares these predictions to empirical biological data, and discusses exceptions in structured/sparse models.
why_interesting: The use of spin-glass-like models (Ising/SK models) and the study of rugged landscapes with epistatic interactions are directly relevant to understanding energy landscapes and optimization in disordered many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
